speaker: interviewer
text: Gisela Konopka, tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis were starting vicious fightings. There was blood all the time, and it wasn't-- and this is before they came to power. I think that's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw, for instance, that kind of thing that happened when they took their spades off and hit people into the face. This is before they come to power. Now, I have to say that we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I better say this is end of 1932, or something like that. But 1933, you know, Hitler is made to be the Vice Chancellor and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we knew that this would be a catastrophe. I mean, that-- unfortunately I have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I forgot to say. Yes, there were already open resistance while they were not yet in power. The labor unions, for instance, had big signs plastered-- you know, what is it called? A 'litfassaule' in Berlin-- L-I-T-F-A-S-S-A-U-L-E. That means big signs of all the labor unions that joined. We must stand against what's coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Kathe Kollwitz-- K-A-T-H-E, Kollwitz, K-O-L-L-W-I-T-Z, was one of the most wonderful artists. I have my whole house is full of them, of her work. Was one of the signers. Unfortunately, the labor movement, the communists split up from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a signer for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For this kind of call, to unite against the terrible coming danger. But unfortunately-- and I regret that very much-- the communists did not join in. They always were separate. They always had this kind of idea it has to come to the worst, then we will win. And that's a dangerous thing that I don't wish anybody ever saying to me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were part of the ISK at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and labor unions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was happening at the meetings that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very strong writing leaflets trying to unite the labor movement. We were very-- and then we became very active in distributing those leaflets against the Nazis. We went to the harbor, and I'm-- you know that Hamburg is a harbor and the people who work in the harbors are very significant. And we went there and stood there and handed out those leaflets against the Nazis. In the early morning, at 5 o'clock before I went to the university. We did it in the evening when, for instance, they were always in the dives-- I can only call them-- where they collected. We went right in there. And I remember we always were sure that there was a man and woman together. And but I still remember that once I lost my partner and a huge-- one of those sailors or whatever they are came next to me and said, you do the right thing, child. I'll protect you. And he went with me. I was no child, but I was little. And it was incredible, the fight that was put up against the Nazis. Not only by the ISK-- the socialists, and the Social Democrats, the unionists. Lots of them, but I wouldn't know about all of them. But this I know because I was part of it. And we thought we can do something, but then '33 comes and Hindenburg-- H-I-N-D-E-N-B-U-R-G-- makes Hitler the Vice Chancellor. At that moment they have all the power they need because Hindenburg actually was feeble-minded at that point. He wasn't quite right there. Then there were other things. We were in Hamburg. Hamburg was always more against the Nazis, but Prussia, where Berlin was, they withdrew. It wasn't that they did everything for the Nazis, but they were-- oh, it won't be so bad. It's always this. Oh, it won't be so bad. And there were many too many people like that. I-- the-- the-- You ask me about my examination. Maybe I start off with that and then I go back to something else, because if I remember right-- but I might be wrong-- it's either January or March 1933 and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] already the Nazis had taken over any significant spots, including the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by taken over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they sent in either the SS, which are the black-- you know, they are in black uniform-- or the SR, SA, who are the brownshirts, and they just are everywhere. They watch everything that everybody does. And they, for instance, had already told-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just mentioned him-- the psychologist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's coming back. They told him that he wouldn't be anymore a professor. All those people who were Jews were thrown out, but he could give the final examinations.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I always tell this because it's strange. The evening before my examination, two SR people come into the room where-- I-- I had only a room. They come into that room and they start looking for any kind of stuff that they could have against me. Was this a room at school or at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. I mean I stayed in a room-- it's not a rooming house. It's something. It's a little-- no, it was a little apartment, actually. It was a little apartment. There are lots of other thing. But I had lots of books, and they pulled them out and they looked for everything. And they made all these remarks. Those dirty Jews, my God. Look at that. All naked people. They are interested in all these naked people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these naked people were Michelangelo. So-- do I have to spell Michelangelo? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But, I mean, this is-- they were stupid at the same time as they were mean. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were sculptures of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Michelangelo. And they were naked, naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyhow, it was a torn up place. But I had to go the next day into my examinations, and I never forget. I took my bicycle. I went first to the washroom and threw up because I was much too upset about a lot of things. And then I went into the examinations.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were held just like they always does-- a long table, the one to be examined on the one side, the professor on the other. But on the two ends of the table, the two brownshirts, watching what we were saying. And I always say it's typical for the-- I sometimes shake my head-- the German bureaucracy that we kept these examinations just the way as if they were real examinations. And I could still tell people what I was asked by William Stern, who was the-- the one in psychology. And then there were others, Flitner-- F-L-I-T-N-E-R-- and Deuchtler-- D-E-U-C-H-T-L-E-R. These are professors who [INAUDIBLE] I had to pass my examinations in education and psychology. I think it was philosophy-- I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the role of the brownshirts who were sitting in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: To watch that we weren't saying anything against Hitler. Now, before I went into the examination room, I have to tell that. Students sit around. One of them very proudly says that she was always a Nazi and thank God she knew all that and this dirty, filthy Jew, that Professor Stern, will be thrown out because he talked about sex in front of good German girls. And anyhow those Jews have to get out. They smell. They are dirty.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I turned to her and I said, you have always sat next to me. You haven't smelled me. Well, you're not Jewish. I said, yes I am. And, in fact, not only that, I come from the worst kind, from the eastern ones. We smell most, don't we?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she got furious. You are saying that. You will be killed because you are against the Nazis. But this is just a little incident, if you want to. This is-- but then I go into the examination room, pass my examinations. I go out, go into one office of one guy that I knew. He wears already the Nazi sign here. I said, my God, you can't-- because I knew him as a socialist. I said, you can't. He said, stop it. We all have to make a living. And this is the word that all my life I was very wary of. I have to make a living, so I don't fight. That was one. Then I went into Flitner and Deuchtler-- these are the two major professors. Flitner says, you will be the genius, the coming genius of our [GERMAN]. And I said, no I won't because I'm Jewish. I will never be that and also I'm against the Nazis. At that time, I still said out loud. I wouldn't have said it perhaps later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the lower grades, because I wanted to be with those kids. And I haven't told you all about being in those schools. I could go for hours but I couldn't. I went to Deuchtler. Now, I think I should say a word because later people say they couldn't understand that I was working with Deuchtler when I was a student because he was a Nazi. Now, that I didn't know, I have to say, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The professor, Deuchtler. He apparently quite early joined the Nazi party. I don't know that, but I'm told that now, OK? Or later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still, I have to say there are people like Deuchtler who I think were the typical professors who think only it's the-- oh, for folk and fatherland, you know? Because when I go in-- first of all, that man helped me to get my money for my examination. But not only that, when I go in after the examination, he says, oh, it's so wonderful. You know, you have it always-- whatever that's called. The top grades. And I said, no, it's not wonderful because I will not because I'm Jewish and I can't. Oh, he says, can't we find a little mouse hole that you can get through?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can see it's almost naive. It's almost stupid. It's this unawareness that I'm very worried about with today about many people. And it's the same man, by the way, who saved another one of the socialists actually from the Nazis, was hiding them. So it's a strange kind of thing we have to know. Anyhow, this is my examination, but I have to-- there's so many things that flood me when I talk about this. I have to say that they are-- the-- the elections that everybody, including people here, will quote that the Nazis won with 90%. I'm very angry that every lie is believed, including that one. Now, we were already organized in some ways and we observed as much as you could. And I know definitely that there were boxes where people had to throw in their-- their ballots-- they were slanted but they had to come in and give their names and then put in the ballot. The moment they were out, they could pull that out, they would know whether they voted yes or no. That's number one. Number two, I knew somebody who was on the people-- on that group that collects the ballots. No, that counts the ballots afterwards. And this man saw that the no votes were thrown in the wastebasket and the yes votes were counted. And he said, we can't do that. Well, they said, you just let it go. On the way back, he was practically killed. He was beaten up and killed-- he wasn't killed. Practically. He was beaten up very badly. All these kind of things went on during those so-called elections and we better know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was voting like that all the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany was a good republic. I mean, there were secret votes, and those ballots looked the same, you know, only that they had yes and no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see of the voting yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stood at the entrance and we saw people going in, but what I've just mentioned is done by people who were observers inside. I couldn't be an observer inside, but that I can only say. So that's true but I don't think they lied to me at that point. That's impossible. Now, I'm not saying that it wasn't 80%. I don't know that. But there was a lot of terror already. The other thing is that I have to say that we have to know that terror started immediately. It didn't start with the war and it didn't start in the beginning only against Jews. It started mostly against socialists, people that they knew were against them. I saw with my own eyes people who came back because they were not allowed-- when they had been arrested, they were not allowed to tell about it. All they did take off their shirts and they were bloody. That's all I can say. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that terror had started in the worst kind. At that time, a man came form Switzerland, who's not alive anymore, and he asked us whether we were willing to really become part of an underground. And it was the ISK that did that. And what would be the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: The job would be to distribute leaflets that were done on very thin paper so that one could swallow it-- I can still swallow paper if it's necessary-- if one can be found. But we should distribute them, which is difficult. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We should collect information, anything we knew, so that they have ammunition against the Nazis. And we should help getting people out of the country, if necessary. Once more, I'm not saying not only Jews, but most Jews and others. And part of the underground were both Jews, Germans, anybody. I have to stress that. I much later, here in this country when I spoke, a young woman came to me who was Jewish and said, I'm so glad I hear you because I thought all Jews at that time were cowards. They did not resist. I said, this is the worst. There were not all-Jewish movements, but there were Jews all the time involved in that. I can-- I named a few, but-- I don't know all the names, but there was Hilda Ehlen, E-H-L-E-N, but her name later was different. And every time when I think of somebody-- Rudy Levy-- L-E-V-Y. I can name them-- though, you know, we forgot names purposely-- that were Jews. I was. Then there were Germans like my husband, who I think has done more courageous things as an anti-Nazi than I ever did because in some ways could do it with his-- as he always said-- unfortunately blue eyes and blond hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not. That is something I will say in a little while. No, but he did incredible things. I give only examples.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I certainly distributed leaflets. And people don't understand what that means. It felt as if they can look through the hole, through the-- through the pocket seeing that, that you carry something, you know? And the way you could hand them out, only shake somebody's hand and push it in their hands. I cannot tell all the details.

speaker: interviewee
text: It sounds like nothing in a country that has free communication. But communication was terribly necessary because we had to let people know that there was a resistance. People think there was no resistance, but there was a resistance. Now, one thing I have to say is that I always say it's one of the worst things that happened in some ways, and that is that one couldn't communicate with anybody one didn't know before the time of the Nazis, which meant that there have been lots of small groups of resistance that never knew from each other. I know an awful lot today more than I knew at that time. We just didn't know from each other. We couldn't. You couldn't talk because you couldn't trust anybody. And I think one of the things that is forgotten about the Nazi period sometimes, besides the cruelty and the torture, is this total destruction of trust and the total, overwhelming feeling of fear. And there is fear. It's not true that we were heroes. We had fear too. Every step you made you thought something would happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have to say that right in the beginning this is there. So when this man came and said would you willing, some of us said yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- they were called [GERMAN] groups, five groups-- groups of five because you couldn't meet with more. When you ever met, you had to just take time to explain why do we meet. There's a birthday, something like that. I'm not going into all of that because maybe that's unimportant, but to us it was very important and it was very courageous. Now, we always said that anybody who is in the underground against the Nazis will live at most three years. It's somehow that we counted three years. That means we said either you get killed right away, you get beaten to death, or you have to leave. But, oddly enough, here I am 86. So it isn't quite, but it was bad enough. And there was one after another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm jumping again, I think, otherwise I-- [INAUDIBLE] What was the next thing that you remembered after handing out leaflets and being part of the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, the next thing? I can only say the arrest-- that's what I want to get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that's the worst. We all knew that we would be arrested. Now, I have to say first the worst thing was that Paul had to leave the country. We knew that they were already in his room watching for him to arrest him. And this is quite a story where I had to-- I was the one who had to accompany him so that he could get to leave the country. Because it was clear that if there was any connection they could make it between us, and if he had to die I could die too. He got-- it was very difficult. And I couldn't tell his mother where he was and I couldn't know for a whole week whether he ever made it across the border. And it's a strange thing when you saw. You go to a movie and then somebody you know whispers to you, isn't that an interesting movie, and then says, he's across the border. I mean, this is the kind of thing-- I cannot go into all these things. It's impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a network.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a network. But, as I once more say, there were very many more networks we didn't know anything about. Anyhow, it's an evening and I meet Rudy Levy, who's not alive anymore and was later an immigrant to some South American country. Was also in the underground. And I meet with him and I say, Rudy, things are very shaky. I want to give you all the information of addresses-- you know, nothing could be written down-- that you have to know because I just don't know what will happen and how long this will go. And so we exercised.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe I should say it. We all have what you call it? 'Galgenhumor,' the humor of the gallows. I still remember that that last night he's called me a joke. And he said, you know in Fuhlsbuttel-- that's the concentration camp in Hamburg that was by the SS, the blackguards. It was the worst.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, somebody said it's terrible. You know, you can't-- it's totally closed in. And the other one says, oh, if I want to get in, I get in. This is the gallows humor that we tell each other. Anyhow, I go home. We had agreed that anything that was of danger, a few addresses, a leaflet or whatever, has to be right next to your bed so that you can swallow it if something happens. In the night the bell rings. There was another joke that went around, all around, that when the bell rings it's not the milkman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the steps come up. I lived in-- with some-- how do you call it? Just a family, and I was just had a little room. The Nazis come in, the blackshirts. Get out. Get dressed. What have I done? You know, you pretend you haven't done anything. I had swallowed that stuff that was wrote down. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They don't even go out of the room. You know, you are young-- I'm still young at that time. I'm 20-- oh, what was I? Something like 25 maybe. And but they stand and look at you, you know, while you're getting dressed, tear everything out of the room and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell a little bit about what happens then. In the night they pick up somebody else and it was another member of our group. And I thought now this is the end. But she was so smart. She immediately start-- she's much younger and she was not Jewish and she was kind of the little working class girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she said, oh, how nice to meet you again. Remember when we were out on vacation? You know, that-- I knew exactly how she would-- what she would say, so that was very good. And she started flirting with those men, which was a smart thing to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, to go into the place that all of us know people will be beaten up. We were put into what I can only describe as standing coffins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's in the Hamburg guardhouse-- the city hall. But it's a cellar, you know? And I might have the wrong building but I think that's about what it was. It's a cellar. There are standing-- I can only describe them like standing wooden coffins. We get pushed in. You cannot turn. You cannot sit. You are completely locked in and it's dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you are in that coffin and that's the first thing what you feel, you know. And I did something that today in psychology they think it's a new invention, which I think is terribly funny to me. But I decided that I'm just not there. I am on a meadow and the sun is shining and that's the way I lived through that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard outside men yelling, I'll tell you anything you want to get me out of here. But it didn't-- as I say, it wasn't heroic. Was just a way of doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very quick. Somebody opened the-- the-- the thing and I have sometimes-- when I wrote this book that I wrote on it, I've said maybe angels wear long beards and are a prison guard. It must've been an old guard who was not a Nazi. And I heard him murmur, why do they do that to young people? And then he bought us something to eat, but time had passed a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very quick. We are put into another room. They bring in people. They beat them. I was not beaten, but I heard many people being beaten. When I turned my head, then I got hit against my head. But I was not otherwise beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will go into the concentration camp.
